teachingPoint: Eligibility checks must run BEFORE audit writes. An ineligible submission that creates an audit record corrupts the audit trail — compliance tools treat audit records as evidence of valid events.
positiveExample: T169 calls eligibilityService.check() at order 1, auditService.storeDocument() at order 4. Ineligible submissions are rejected before any write occurs.
negativeExample: T169 writes auditService.storeDocument() first (FLOW-08 pattern), then checks eligibility. An ineligible attempt now has an audit record claiming a review was submitted.
discriminatingConstraint: executionOrder: eligibility=1, validation=2, setnx=3, audit=4, emit=5. Any model that places audit before eligibility violates IR-1 and scores 0.
keywords: eligibility before audit order constraint ineligible submission audit record corrupt compliance FLOW-08 inversion

teachingPoint: Rating validation must run BEFORE the SETNX idempotency check. Consuming an idempotency slot for an obviously invalid rating (0, 6, null) prevents the user from submitting a corrected review later.
positiveExample: T169 validates rating [1,5] at order 2, calls SETNX at order 3. Invalid rating emits ReviewRejected(invalid_rating) before any idempotency key is consumed.
negativeExample: T169 calls SETNX first, then validates rating. A submission with rating=6 consumes the idempotency slot — the user cannot submit a valid review for the same entity.
discriminatingConstraint: SETNX at position 3, validation at position 2. Idempotency keys are a finite resource; don't consume them on inputs that fail validation.
keywords: validation before SETNX idempotency key consumed invalid rating slot finite resource

teachingPoint: Moderation must have three outcomes: PASS, REJECT, and UNCERTAIN. UNCERTAIN routes to a human queue (PENDING state), never auto-rejects. Binary moderation silently discards valid borderline content.
positiveExample: T170 evaluates confidence against two thresholds. Above pass threshold → ReviewPublished. Below reject threshold → ReviewRejected. Between thresholds → ReviewFlaggedForHuman with PENDING status.
negativeExample: T170 uses a single threshold: above → publish, below → reject. UNCERTAIN submissions are silently rejected — borderline but valid content is permanently lost.
discriminatingConstraint: UNCERTAIN maps to ReviewFlaggedForHuman with status=PENDING, not ReviewRejected. The review can still be published after human review.
keywords: three path moderation uncertain human queue PENDING binary collapse FLOW-08 conflict auto-reject

teachingPoint: Aggregation services that affect reputation must subscribe to retraction events, not just addition events. Additive-only aggregation permanently inflates scores when reviews are retracted.
positiveExample: T171 has two @EventPattern handlers: ReviewPublished (add path) and ReviewRetracted (remove path). Both call recalculate(), which fetches all PUBLISHED reviews and recomputes.
negativeExample: T171 only subscribes to ReviewPublished. When a reviewer retracts their 5-star review, the reputation score remains inflated — the retraction is silently ignored.
discriminatingConstraint: Score recalculation must re-fetch all PUBLISHED reviews (status=PUBLISHED filter), not use a running counter. Retracted reviews must not appear in the filtered set.
keywords: retraction aggregation additive subtract remove review score inflation published filter recalculate

teachingPoint: Reputation scores on a [1.0, 5.0] scale require explicit clamping before write. Weighted averages near the boundaries can produce values below 1.0 or above 5.0 under certain weight configurations.
positiveExample: T171 computes rawScore = weightedAverage(...), then applies Math.max(1.0, Math.min(5.0, rawScore)) before storeDocument. The written score is always within [1.0, 5.0].
negativeExample: T171 writes rawScore directly. Under heavy recency weighting with sparse data, the weighted average can produce 0.8 or 5.3. Downstream consumers receive out-of-range scores.
discriminatingConstraint: Score range [1.0, 5.0] is a star-rating scale, not a quality score [0,1]. Named check reputation_score_clamped_1_to_5 enforces this. Do not confuse with FLOW-06's [0,1] scale.
keywords: score clamp reputation scale 1.0 5.0 weighted average float escape named check star rating

teachingPoint: revision_allowed in rejection events must be scoped to the rejection reason. content_policy rejections allow one revision; ownership and duplicate rejections do not.
positiveExample: T172 emits ReviewResponseRejected with revision_allowed:true only for content_policy violations. not_owner and already_responded rejections carry revision_allowed:false.
negativeExample: T172 always sets revision_allowed:true on every rejection. A non-owner is told they may revise their response — which will be rejected again for ownership, consuming another idempotency slot.
discriminatingConstraint: revision_allowed is a discriminating field in the rejection event payload. The caller uses it to decide whether to present a revision UI. Wrong values cause misleading UX.
keywords: conditional revision allowed content policy not owner already responded rejection payload discriminating

teachingPoint: reviewId is a server-derived deterministic hash, never a client-supplied field. Accepting reviewId from the event payload allows callers to target specific idempotency slots or overwrite existing records.
positiveExample: T169 computes reviewId = hash(tenantId+':'+reviewerId+':'+targetEntityId+':'+targetEntityType) server-side. The incoming event carries only reviewerId, targetEntityId, targetEntityType, and rating.
negativeExample: T169 reads reviewId from event.reviewId. A caller can supply a known reviewId and overwrite an existing review record, or supply a fabricated ID to bypass idempotency.
discriminatingConstraint: The SETNX key and the document ID are the same server-derived hash. If SETNX returns false, the review already exists — no separate document ID lookup needed.
keywords: server derived reviewId hash deterministic caller trust idempotency SETNX same key document ID

teachingPoint: FREEDOM config thresholds must not be hardcoded in moderation logic. Hardcoded thresholds require a code deployment to adjust moderation sensitivity, making A/B testing and incident response impossible.
positiveExample: T170 reads flow10_moderation_pass_threshold and flow10_moderation_reject_threshold from xiigen-freedom-config at runtime. Changing moderation sensitivity requires only a config update.
negativeExample: T170 hardcodes const PASS_THRESHOLD = 0.85. Adjusting moderation sensitivity in response to a spam wave requires a deployment cycle.
discriminatingConstraint: FREEDOM config key: 'flow10_moderation_pass_threshold'. The key name is a MACHINE constant; the value is FREEDOM-configurable.
keywords: FREEDOM config threshold moderation hardcoded externalized runtime config A/B sensitivity

teachingPoint: ReviewSubmissionCompleted fires on T169 acceptance (ReviewAccepted), not on T170 moderation pass (ReviewPublished). The submitter's synchronous wait ends when eligibility and validity are confirmed — moderation is time-decoupled.
positiveExample: T169 emits ReviewSubmissionCompleted alongside ReviewAccepted. The client receives reviewId + status:'ACCEPTED'. Whether the review is published or flagged for human review happens asynchronously.
negativeExample: ReviewSubmissionCompleted is emitted after T170's ReviewPublished. The submitter waits for moderation to complete (potentially minutes) before receiving their submission acknowledgment.
discriminatingConstraint: ReviewSubmissionCompleted is the response contract — it confirms the submission was processed. ReviewPublished is a separate asynchronous signal that the content passed moderation.
keywords: completion event ReviewSubmissionCompleted ReviewAccepted sync async moderation decoupled submitter wait

teachingPoint: A shared factory (F172 IReviewAggregateService) injected into two services (T171 write, T172 read) must be registered once in the NestJS module — not independently in each service's module.
positiveExample: reviews-reputation.module.ts registers F172 as a single provider. T172's module imports it from T171's provider registration. One service instance, consistent state.
negativeExample: T172's module independently registers a new F172 provider. Two separate service instances exist with potentially inconsistent aggregate caches. Module boundary violations may occur.
discriminatingConstraint: SHARED READ SERVICE label in COMPLETE FLOW ASSEMBLY marks factories with multiple consumers. Single NestJS provider registration is the enforcement mechanism.
keywords: shared factory provider registration NestJS module boundary single instance aggregate service
eligibility before audit write order constraint ineligible attempt corrupt audit trail FLOW-08 inversion IR-1
ironRules: eligibility GET is executionOrder=1 — before any write, before SETNX, before validation
Ineligible submissions never create audit records — audit trail records valid events only
If peer flow (FLOW-04, FLOW-09) not ACTIVE → ReviewRejected(peer_flow_unavailable), no audit
This pattern INVERTS the FLOW-08 audit-first-then-eligibility order — both are domain-correct
DPO triple annotation: conflictsWith: 'FLOW-08-T79-audit-first-pattern'

keywords: three path moderation PASS REJECT UNCERTAIN human queue PENDING state binary collapse FLOW-08 conflict moderationPaths
ironRules: Exactly 3 moderation outcomes: PASS → ReviewPublished, REJECT → ReviewRejected, UNCERTAIN → ReviewFlaggedForHuman
UNCERTAIN maps to PENDING status — never to REJECTED status
A PENDING review can be published after human decision — it is not a final rejection
Two FREEDOM config thresholds: pass_threshold and reject_threshold — between them is UNCERTAIN
DPO triple annotation: conflictsWith FLOW-08 binary moderation (confidence < threshold → REJECT)

keywords: retraction aggregation ReviewRetracted remove recalculate PUBLISHED filter additive subtractive first in system
ironRules: Subscribe to both ReviewPublished (add) and ReviewRetracted (remove)
Both event handlers call the same recalculate() function — not a running counter
Score recalculation fetches all PUBLISHED reviews (status=PUBLISHED filter) — flagged/pending excluded
First additive-subtractive pattern in XIIGen — prior flows (FLOW-05/06) are additive-only
Named check: retraction_removes_from_aggregate

keywords: score clamp 1.0 5.0 star rating scale weighted average float boundary Math.min Math.max named check
ironRules: Score range [1.0, 5.0] — a star-rating scale, NOT a quality score [0,1] (distinct from FLOW-06)
rawScore = weightedAverage(publishedRatings, recencyWeights)
score = Math.max(1.0, Math.min(5.0, rawScore)) — clamp before any write
Recency weights from FREEDOM config 'flow10_reputation_recency_weights' — never hardcoded
Named check: reputation_score_clamped_1_to_5

keywords: conditional revision allowed content policy only not owner already responded rejection event payload discriminating field
ironRules: revision_allowed:true is emitted ONLY for content_policy rejections
not_owner rejection → revision_allowed:false — the user has no review to respond to
already_responded rejection → revision_allowed:false — SETNX key is already consumed
revision_allowed is a required field on all ReviewResponseRejected events
Revision strategy (SETNX bypass mechanism) is a PRODUCT DECISION — read from STATE.json
